cataloging_subject.label: English Wycliffite Sermons: Sunday Gospels and Festivals
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloging unit is the exact 123-sermon Middle English selection printed in Thomas Arnold's 1869 volume I and encoded as U-M item AFB3713.0001.001: fifty-four Sunday Gospel sermons, thirty-one Commune Sanctorum sermons, and thirty-eight Proprium Sanctorum sermons. These are three liturgically coherent portions of the unified 294-sermon long English Wycliffite cycle, not 123 separately attributed works and not the whole corpus of writings historically assigned to John Wyclif. Arnold's title page, introduction, contents, marginal analyses, biblical references, notes, supplied punctuation, and other apparatus remain edition-level matter rather than medieval component works.

catalog_summary.title: English Wycliffite Sermons: Sunday Gospels and Festivals
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous Wycliffite author or authors
catalog_summary.date: Late fourteenth or early fifteenth century; Arnold selection published 1869
catalog_summary.region: England; Wycliffite or Lollard textual culture

titles.value: English Wycliffite Sermons: Sunday Gospels and Festivals
notes: A supplied modern title that combines the established cycle name with the exact Arnold volume-I boundary; it is not claimed as a medieval rubric.

titles.value: Select English Works of John Wyclif, Volume I
notes: The immediate edition title preserves Arnold's historical attribution; it is not adopted as proof of Wyclif's authorship.

titles.value: Sermons on the Gospels for Sundays and Festivals

agents.name: John Wyclif
notes: Traditional and Arnold-era attribution; modern scholarship does not establish his sole authorship of the long sermon cycle.

agents.name: Thomas Arnold
notes: Editor of Select English Works of John Wyclif; responsible for the selection, organization, punctuation, capitalization, paragraphing, marginal analyses, biblical references, and apparatus.

agents.name: W. F. Cornish
notes: Prepared the manuscript transcript used for Arnold's edition through printed p. 197.

agents.name: William Sorell
notes: Prepared the remainder of the manuscript transcript used for Arnold's edition after printed p. 197.

scope.description: All 123 sermons as selected from the unified long cycle

notes: Cycle unity is supported, but the reviewed evidence does not resolve single versus collaborative authorship.

scope.description: Historical attribution of all three sermon sections

assertion.notes: Supported as reception and edition history, not as confirmed authorship.

notes: Arnold argued for Wyclif's authorship; current scholarship instead describes a cycle produced by Wyclif's followers and leaves its authorship unresolved.

scope.description: The complete 1869 volume-I selection and editorial apparatus
Arnold printed pp. 1-197: all Sunday Gospel sermons and Commune Sanctorum through the beginning of Sermon LXIII
Arnold printed pp. 198-412: the remainder of Commune Sanctorum and all Proprium Sanctorum
Selected variants and corrections from manuscripts B, C, D, and E, plus passage checks in Royal 18 B IX and Cotton Claudius D VIII

date_statements.display: Late fourteenth or early fifteenth century

assertion.notes: The deliberately broad range does not depend on Arnold's assumption of Wyclif's personal authorship or transfer a witness-copying date to composition.

notes: Arnold's narrower arguments from Richard II and persecution references belong to the history of attribution and dating, not a settled modern composition date.

scope.description: First publication of Arnold's exact volume-I edited selection

places.label: Wycliffite or Lollard textual culture
notes: A cultural association, not a precise place of composition.
Witness origins, scribal dialects, and present repositories are not transferred automatically to the work's place of composition.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: Latin Gospel lemmata, rubrics, and short phrases are embedded formulae and are not promoted to a separate work-level language facet.

form_statements.description: All 123 medieval sermons are prose. The XML's five LG and twenty-six L elements occur inside Arnold's modern editorial notes and do not make the cataloged selection mixed-form.

summaries.value: This 123-sermon selection expounds Gospel readings for Sundays, the Common of Saints, and the Proper of Saints in Middle English. Sermons normally begin from a Latin Gospel lemma, explain its narrative and spiritual senses, and apply scripture to belief, sin and virtue, preaching, pastoral conduct, wealth and poverty, sacraments, clergy and friars, papal authority, Antichrist, and reform of the church. The three sections belong to the larger 294-sermon long English Wycliffite cycle.

Part I contains fifty-four Sunday Gospel sermons (Arnold I-LIV, pp. 1-163). Part II contains thirty-one Commune Sanctorum sermons (LV-LXXXV, pp. 165-294). Part III contains thirty-eight Proprium Sanctorum sermons (LXXXVI-CXXIII, pp. 295-412).

The record preserves Arnold's volume-I boundary because afb3713 encodes that exact selection, while keeping the intellectual cycle, manuscript witnesses, 1869 edition, U-M encoding, and checked-in XML distinct. John Wyclif remains a disputed attributed author rather than the confirmed creator.

extent.description: The medieval selection comprises 54 Sunday Gospel sermons, 31 Commune Sanctorum sermons, and 38 Proprium Sanctorum sermons. The complete XML has five DIV1 elements (two omitted-front-matter containers and three medieval content sections), 123 DIV2, 252 HEAD, 499 P, 448 PB, 798 NOTE1 plus three header NOTE elements, five LG, and twenty-six L. All five line groups and all twenty-six line elements are modern editorial quotations inside Arnold's notes, so the medieval selection remains prose.

content_parts.label: Evangelia Dominicalia: Sunday Gospels
content_parts.summary: Fifty-four sermons on Sunday Gospel readings, ordered from the first Sunday after Trinity through Trinity Sunday.

content_parts.label: Commune Sanctorum
content_parts.summary: Thirty-one sermons, numbered LV-LXXXV, for Gospel readings in the Common of Saints.

content_parts.label: Proprium Sanctorum
content_parts.summary: Thirty-eight sermons, numbered LXXXVI-CXXIII, for saints' days and other festivals from St Andrew's vigil through All Saints' Day.

related_works.title: English Wycliffite Sermons (the 294-sermon long cycle)
notes: The 123 sermons cataloged here are a coherent Arnold selection from the long cycle, not its complete 294-sermon extent.

evidence.locator: title page; contents pp. xxiii-xxix; printed pp. 1-412
evidence.summary: Immediate-edition evidence for title, editor, publication, exact three-section selection, sermon numbering, pagination, section headings, textual contents, and Arnold's notes and apparatus.

evidence.citation: Thomas Arnold, introduction and manuscript account in Select English Works of John Wyclif, vol. I (Oxford: Clarendon Press, 1869), pp. i-xxii.
evidence.summary: Editor-authored evidence for Arnold's historical attribution and dating arguments, cycle unity and arrangement, manuscript sigla, Bodley 788 base text, selected collation and passage checks, orthographic policy, supplied capitalization and punctuation, editorial apparatus, and Cornish/Sorell transcription responsibilities.

evidence.citation: Anne Hudson and Pamela Gradon, eds., English Wycliffite Sermons, vol. 1 (Oxford: Clarendon Press, 1983).
evidence.summary: Modern edition evidence that the complete long cycle was produced by followers of Wyclif and that volume I contains dominical Gospel and Epistle sermons; used for modern title, cycle boundaries, cultural attribution, and caution about personal authorship.

evidence.citation: Kantik Ghosh, 'The English Wycliffite Sermons: Thinking in Alternatives?', in Wycliffite Heresy: Authority and the Interpretation of Texts (Cambridge University Press, 2001), DOI 10.1017/CBO9780511483288.005.
evidence.summary: Current scholarly framing of the 294-sermon long cycle as a unified whole whose single or multiple authorship remains obscure and for which collaborative production is plausible.

evidence.citation: Middle English Compendium bibliography entry BIB3873, English Wycliffite Sermons, ed. Anne Hudson and Pamela Gradon, five volumes (1983-1996).
evidence.summary: Current bibliography and witness-census evidence for the modern five-volume edition, surviving manuscript shelfmarks, approximate witness dates, and microfilm references.

evidence.citation: General Corpus source-lineage manifest for afb3713.
evidence.summary: Linked evidence for distinct repository, encoding, edition, facsimile, catalog, and manuscript layers; exact transcription and collation scopes; local-cache inventory; access routes; and component-specific rights.

open_questions.question: What single-author, collaborative, compilatory, or revision model best explains the unified 294-sermon long cycle, and what relationship did John Wyclif have to its composition?
next_steps: Consult the complete Hudson-Gradon five-volume edition and later scholarship rather than using Arnold's attribution as the critical baseline.
Separate evidence for cycle unity from evidence for one author, several authors, revision, or compilation.
Retain manuscript and edition attributions as reception evidence without converting them into confirmed authorship.

open_questions.question: Can the three selected sermon sections be dated more narrowly without depending on Wyclif's personal authorship or transferring dates from surviving copies?
next_steps: Review the textual and historical dating arguments across all five Hudson-Gradon volumes.
Reassess Arnold's Richard II and persecution evidence independently of his authorship conclusion.
Keep witness production dates distinct from composition.

notes: The source header's broad three-volume bibliographic description is inherited catalog metadata; the encoded title page and content identify the exact immediate source as Arnold volume I (1869).

The XML preserves page milestones for twenty-nine omitted introductory and contents pages but does not transcribe their wording; those omissions are edition-layer boundaries, not missing medieval sermons.
The XML title-page attribution to John Wyclif is edition-derived and is not treated as proof of authorship.
The record remains partial because authorship and narrower composition dating remain unresolved.
